summary: O'Neil reviews a legal paper by Barocas and Selbst examining whether anti-discrimination law can address unintentional bias in big data models. The paper concludes that Title VII is largely inadequate for handling algorithmic discrimination, and reform faces significant political and constitutional barriers. O'Neil adds her own experience from advertising: modelers don't track discriminatory effects on users, only profit gains. She argues that the dehumanization inherent in modeling prevents awareness of discrimination as an issue, making current legal frameworks even more insufficient than the paper suggests.
key_points: Current anti-discrimination law (Title VII) is ill-equipped to address unintentional discrimination from data mining
Training models on historical data can codify past discriminatory treatment without anyone intending or noticing it
Modelers optimize for profit, not user outcomes, so discriminatory effects go unmeasured
The assumption that builders will 'notice' discriminatory treatment is naive—in practice, users are not humanized
Legal reform faces both technical challenges within data mining and political/constitutional constraints
money_quotes: The short answer is no, our laws are not equipped.
We don't measure the effects of our models on our users. We only see whether we have gained an edge in terms of profit, which is very different.
As modelers, we don't humanize the people on the other side of the transaction, which prevents us from worrying about discrimination or even being aware of it as an issue.
It's so far from 'intentional' that it's almost a ridiculous accusation to make.
I don't know how we as a society can deal with it unless we update our laws.
key_insight: Algorithmic discrimination often goes undetected not because it's hidden, but because modelers are optimizing for profit and never measure their impact on the humans affected.
